curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: angles on parallel lines (alternate, corresponding, co-interior)
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Identify and use alternate and corresponding angle equalities and co-interior angle sums to solve unknown-angle problems and to justify when lines are parallel.

verse_ality.driving_question: What relationships exist between the angles formed when a transversal cuts two parallel lines?

knowing_world.focus: Explore angle relationships created by a transversal cutting parallel lines and apply them to find unknown angles and test whether lines are parallel.
key_concepts: Angle facts and reasoning to prove lines are parallel

human_story.focus: Connect angle relationships on parallel lines to real-world alignment in architecture, transport networks, and design (tracks, facades, road markings) where checking parallelism matters.
justice_prompts: Why is geometric accuracy useful in real-world design, and who is affected when alignment is wrong?

expressive_self.focus: Represent and explain the relationships using drawings, colour, storytelling and voice, building confidence to identify ‘Z’ and ‘F’ patterns and supplementary pairs.
activities: Create a drawing of two parallel lines and a transversal — colour matched pairs of equal angles (alternate, corresponding, co-interior).
Record a short explanation (voice-note or video) describing how you identify the “Z” angles and the “F” angles in such a diagram.
Invent a short comic where a “line” character meets a “transversal” and discovers angle friends — alternate, corresponding, co-interior.

designed_world.focus: Use a web sketchpad to drag a transversal across parallel lines, observe equalities and supplementary pairs, and test what changes when lines stop being parallel.
design_challenges: Move the transversal and observe how the corresponding angle matches the original when the lines remain parallel.
Drag to show the “Z” shape (alternate interior angles) — describe what you notice about the angles marked.
Create a pair of co-interior angles and check they sum to 180°.
Challenge: What happens if the lines are no longer parallel? Which relationships break down?

regulation_supports: Stand straight holding two long ropes as parallel lines. A friend walks across them with a stick acting as a transversal. At each intersection count the angles created and match “Z” shapes or “F” shapes with gestures.

pathways.name: Final Task: Angle Relationships Poster/Slide
pathways.focus: Create a mini-poster or slide showing two parallel lines and a transversal with all key angle pairs labelled (alternate, corresponding, co-interior), stating which are equal or supplementary and linking to a real-life application.
products: A clear diagram with all key angle pairs labelled (alternate, corresponding, co-interior).
Annotations showing which angles are equal and which sum to 180° (e.g., ∠a = ∠b, ∠c + ∠d = 180°).
A short real-life application note (e.g., road markings, ladder rails, building frame alignment).
A brief reflection: which relationship was easiest, which needed more reasoning, and why?
criteria: Understanding — Correctly classify each angle pair and state the relationship.
Fluency & Accuracy — Accurately label angles and include equation form.
Application — Explain how this is used in real life.
Independence & Reflection — Identify what was easiest/hardest and why.

required: Angle facts on a straight line (180°)
Basic naming/identification of angles in diagrams

builds_toward: Angles in a triangle (parallel line proof pathway)
Reasoning and proof in geometry problems
